articles.title: Punk Rock Chicks? These Baby Birds Just Won a Major Photo Contest
articles.summary: Three baby egrets with spiky feathers that look like tiny mohawks have won the grand prize in Smithsonian Magazine's 23rd annual photography contest. Jacqueline Burke, a 64-year-old veterinarian, captured the chicks squabbling at a rookery in New Jersey. The contest received more than 17,000 submissions from 108 countries. Other winners include a woman in a black veil, a Navajo teenager on a horse, a chameleon, and a circular building in China. The photos celebrate everything from wildlife to culture to architecture.

articles.title: Peacocks vs. People: A California Town's Feathered Frenzy
articles.summary: Arcadia, California, has a unique problem: dozens of peacocks roam the streets, yards, and rooftops. Introduced by businessman Elias Lucky Baldwin over a century ago, the birds are now protected by local law. A new short documentary, "Our Neighbors, the Peacocks," explores the divide between residents who love the birds and those who want them gone. The film resists offering a simple solution, instead asking whether humans can coexist with nature's more challenging creatures.

articles.title: Venice Biennale Jury Refuses to Award Russia and Israel Over ICC Charges
articles.summary: The all-women jury for the 61st Venice Biennale has announced it will not consider countries whose leaders are charged by the International Criminal Court (ICC) for awards. Though unnamed, this targets Russia (President Putin) and Israel (PM Netanyahu), both facing ICC arrest warrants for war crimes and crimes against humanity. The decision follows pressure from artists and activists, with nearly 10,000 signing a letter against Russia's return. The European Union has also threatened to suspend a €2 million grant over Russia's participation.
